title: The City of Brass
summary: Caliph Abd al-Malik sends Emir Musa and his party across the Sahara to find the legendary City of Brass, built by King Solomon to imprison rebellious jinn. They encounter a brass horseman statue that points the way, a chained Ifrit imprisoned in a pillar, and an ancient city with walls eighty cubits high and twenty-five gates, eerily preserved but inhabited only by corpses. Inside lies the embalmed princess Tadmurah of the Amalekites, her treasure guarded by animated statues. Greedy Talib ignores warnings and is decapitated by guardian statues. The party retrieves brass bottles containing imprisoned jinn and returns to Damascus.

source_references.title: The Thousand and One Nights (Alf Layla wa-Layla): From the Earliest Known Sources
source_references.author: Husain Haddawy (trans.)

source_references.title: The City of Brass: From the Arabian Nights
source_references.author: Husain Haddawy

full_text:
Emir Musa stood at the edge of the Sahara and looked east, toward the place where the sand turned from gold to brass in the dying light, and he knew that the journey ahead would cost him more than the blisters on his feet and the thirst in his throat. Caliph Abd al-Malik had sent him to find the City of Brass, the legendary fortress built by King Solomon to imprison the rebellious jinn, and Musa was not the kind of man who turned back from a commission, however dangerous.

The caravan moved through the desert for weeks. They found, in a valley between two dunes, a brass horseman mounted on a pillar, its finger pointing east. They followed the direction and came to a well from which rose the sound of chains rattling, and when they looked down, they saw an Ifrit bound in iron, his face twisted with rage, who told them of the city and begged for release. Musa refused, for he knew that the jinni's gratitude was less dangerous than its freedom.

The city itself was vast and terrible in its beauty. The walls rose eighty cubits high, pierced by twenty-five gates of worked brass, and inside the streets were laid out with a precision that spoke of a mind not human. The houses stood empty but clean, as if their inhabitants had stepped out moments before. In the center of the city lay a palace of such magnificence that Musa's scribes wept at the sight of it, for they knew that no living craftsman could match its splendor.

In the throne room they found the body of Princess Tadmurah of the Amalekites, embalmed and seated upon a throne of gold, her treasures heaped around her in mountains of coin and gemstone. Beside her stood animated statues -- brass figures with drawn swords that turned slowly, guarding what remained of a kingdom that had defied God. One of Musa's companions, a greedy man named Talib, reached for a jewel and the statues moved, and Talib died, and his blood was the first to stain the floor of the palace in a thousand years.

Musa took what he could carry and left the rest, and when he returned to Damascus, the caliph listened to his account and wept, for the City of Brass was a lesson in the vanity of earthly power, and even a caliph needed to be reminded of that now and then.

title: The Contendings of Horus and Set
summary: The epic struggle between Horus, the son of Osiris, and Set, the murderer of Osiris, for the throne of Egypt is one of the most dramatic myths from ancient Egypt. After Osiris descended to the underworld, his son Horus came of age and demanded the kingship that was rightfully his. Set, Osiris's brother, refused to yield. The case was brought before the Ennead, the council of gods, presided over by Geb or Ra. The trial lasted eighty years. The conflict was both legal and violent: the gods debated endlessly while Horus and Set engaged in terrible battles. Set tore out Horus's eye; snip Horus castrated Set. Isis used cunning and magic to aid her son, at one point transforming into a beautiful woman to trick Set into condemning himself. Thoth served as scribe and mediator. Eventually the gods ruled in Horus's favor, and he became king of Egypt. The myth established the principle of divine kingship: every pharaoh was considered the living Horus. Set was not destroyed but was given dominion over the desert and foreign lands, representing chaos kept at bay by order.
source: Wikipedia - Osiris myth; snip Pinch - Handbook of Egyptian Mythology
source_references: Shirley, Greg. The Ancient Egyptian Contendings of Horus and Seth. In Ancient Egyptian Literature: Theory and Practice, edited by Roland Enmarch and Vernus. Cambridge University Press, 2013; Lichtheim, Miriam. Ancient Egyptian Literature. Vol. 1. University of California Press, 1973
full_text:
After Osiris was murdered by his brother Set and his body scattered across the land, his wife Isis gathered the pieces and gave him a proper burial. But Osiris had left behind a son: Horus, the falcon-headed child of divine right, who grew to manhood in the marshes of the Delta nursing a single, burning desire—to claim the throne that Set had stolen. When Horus came before the Ennead, the great council of gods, and demanded judgment, Set laughed. He had ruled Egypt for decades, and he was not inclined to surrender his crown to a nephew barely old enough to grow a beard. The trial that followed lasted eighty years, the longest lawsuit in the history of heaven and earth.

The gods debated endlessly. Ra, growing old and weary, presided with diminishing patience. Isis, the most cunning of the goddesses, used every stratagem at her disposal to win the case for her son. She transformed herself into a beautiful woman and lured Set into a trap, coaxing him to declare that a young man's claim to his father's estate was just—then revealing Horus as that young man. Set raged and attacked Horus, tearing out his eye in a savage battle. Horus retaliated, severing Set's testicles and driving him into the desert. Thoth, the ibis-headed scribe, recorded every word and every blow, his reed pen flying across the papyrus of destiny. At last the gods ruled in Horus's favor. He was crowned king of Egypt, and every pharaoh who followed would be called the Living Horus—the falcon on the throne, order restored from chaos. Set was not destroyed but given dominion over the desert and foreign lands, for the Egyptians understood that chaos, though it must be contained, can never be entirely eliminated.

title: The Clever Girl of Kairouan
summary: A Tunisian folktale from the ancient city of Kairouan about a poor man and his brilliant daughter. When famine strikes, the man seeks help from a corrupt judge who sets an impossible condition: 'You must come to me neither dressed nor undressed, neither walking nor riding, neither by day nor by night.' The man's daughter solves the riddle: she wraps herself in a fishing net so she is neither clothed nor naked, rides a donkey with her feet dragging so she is neither walking nor riding, and arrives at dawn—the moment between night and day. When the judge tries to swindle her father, she outsmarts him in court with her quick wit, exposing his corruption and securing justice for her family. The story became famous throughout Tunisia as a symbol of how intellect and moral courage can overcome corrupt authority, and is still told to emphasize the values of wisdom and justice in Maghrebi oral tradition.
source: FolktalesAfrica.com - The Clever Girl of Kairouan
source_references: Cardaire, Marie-Joséph. L'Artisanat artistique au Togo. Mision catholique, 1965; Leloup, Henri. Art et mythologie du Togo. Centre culturel français, 1966
full_text:
In the ancient holy city of Kairouan, where the Great Mosque cast its shadow across the market squares, there lived a poor man and his daughter. When famine came to the land and the grain bins emptied one by one, the father sought help from the qadi, a judge known for his wealth but also for his cruelty. The qadi, seeing an opportunity to exploit the helpless man, set a condition so impossible that no mortal could meet it: "Come to me neither dressed nor undressed, neither walking nor riding, neither by day nor by night, and I will give you what you need."

The father went home in despair. But his daughter, a young woman whose mind was sharp as a needle, smiled when she heard the riddle. At dawn—the hour that belongs neither to night nor to day—she wrapped herself in a fishing net so that she was neither clothed nor naked, and she rode a donkey with her feet dragging in the dust so that she was neither walking nor riding. When she arrived at the qadi's door, he was speechless. He tried to cheat her father in the court anyway, withholding the promised aid on a technicality. But the girl spoke up, her voice calm and clear, and dismantled the qadi's argument piece by piece before the assembled townspeople. His corruption was exposed, the crowd murmured with indignation, and the girl walked out with her father's dignity restored and a purse of coins pressed into her hand by the shame-faced judge. The people of Kairouan still tell this story when they want to remind one another that the cleverest mind does not belong to the man with the most power, but to the person with the most justice in their heart.
